raw_case:
Unilateral adrenal Castleman's disease: A case report
Castleman disease (CD) is a heterogeneous hematological condition characterized by distinctive histopathological features. The etiology remains unclear, and clinical symptoms are generally nonspecific. CD can occur in any location containing lymphatic tissue, with the mediastinum being the most common site, while adrenal involvement is rare. Adrenal CD is typically incidentally discovered during physical examination, commonly affecting one side. Imaging studies often make it challenging to differentiate from common adrenal tumors, necessitating pathological confirmation. Surgical intervention is the preferred treatment, and the prognosis is generally favorable.This paper presents a rare case of a left adrenal mass, which was diagnosed as Castleman disease following surgical resection.1

Introduction

Castleman disease (CD) is a heterogeneous hematological condition characterized by distinctive histopathological features, first described by Castleman in 1956, representing a complex lymphoproliferative disorder.1 The etiology remains unclear, and clinical symptoms are generally nonspecific. CD can occur in any location containing lymphatic tissue, with the mediastinum being the most common site, while adrenal involvement is rare. Adrenal CD is typically incidentally discovered during physical examination, commonly affecting one side.2 Imaging studies often make it challenging to differentiate from common adrenal tumors, necessitating pathological confirmation. Surgical intervention is the preferred treatment, and the prognosis is generally favorable.This paper presents a rare case of a left adrenal mass, which was diagnosed as Castleman disease following surgical resection.2

Case report

The patient is a 50-year-old male with no history of hypertension, diabetes, or previous abdominal surgeries. In March 2023, he was referred to our hospital after a left adrenal mass was discovered during a routine examination at a lower-level hospital. Upon admission, the patient underwent a complete abdominal CT scan, which revealed a left retroperitoneal mass measuring approximately 55mm × 40mm. The mass had well-defined margins, with multiple calcifications and mild enhancement observed (Fig. 1 A, B, C). Other imaging studies showed no lymphadenopathy or additional tumors. The patient underwent dynamic blood pressure monitoring, which indicated no hypertension. Additional laboratory tests, including complete blood count, biochemistry, coagulation, and myocardial injury markers, showed no significant abnormalities. Adrenal hormone assessments, including renin, aldosterone, 24-h urinary vanillylmandelic acid, catecholamines, ARR, and cortisol, were all within normal ranges.After completing the evaluation, the patient underwent laparoscopic resection of the left retroperitoneal mass. Intraoperatively, the retroperitoneal tumor exhibited mild adhesion to surrounding tissues. The surgery was successful, and the resected specimen was light brown, firm, and measured 60mm × 42mm x 35mm. Upon sectioning, the tumor revealed a pale yellow tissue (Fig. 1 D). Postoperative pathological examination and immunohistochemical results showed: CD3 (++), CD20 (++), CD31 (+), CD34 (vascular +), KI-67 (10 % +), Vimentin (−), CD5 (+), Cyclin D1 (focal +), BCL-2 (non-germinal center +), and BCL-6 (germinal center +). Hematoxylin and eosin staining revealed small vessel proliferation and thickened vessel walls, with a central region containing transparent and sclerotic vessels surrounded by concentric layers of small lymphocytes (Fig. 1E and F). Based on the HE staining and immunohistochemical results, the final diagnosis was para-adrenal Castleman disease (giant lymph node hyperplasia) on the left side. The patient was discharged smoothly 5 days post-surgery without complications. Three months after discharge, a follow-up abdominal CT revealed no tumor recurrence at the surgical site or other locations, and all laboratory tests showed no significant abnormalities, indicating a favorable surgical outcome.3

Discussion

Castleman disease (lymphoproliferative disorder) is a rare benign lymphoproliferative condition. Epidemiological studies indicate an incidence rate of approximately 2125 cases per million, with a median age of onset between 34 and 40 years. Castleman disease (CD) can affect multiple locations, typically involving lymph nodes in the neck, abdomen, and mediastinum, with rare occurrences in the retroperitoneal adrenal region.3 The pathogenesis of adrenal Castleman disease remains unclear; however, several theories exist, including abnormal expression of interleukin (IL)-6 and elevated levels of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) associated with viral stimulation and angiogenesis. Due to the lack of non-specific clinical manifestations and imaging features, early diagnosis of adrenal Castleman disease is often challenging. Such cases are typically discovered during physical examinations, with definitive diagnosis reliant on pathological and immunohistochemical results.Generally, large retroperitoneal and adrenal masses require assessment of adrenal-related hormones to aid in determining the nature of the tumor. In cases of adrenal Castleman disease, most do not present with significant adrenal insufficiency or excess hormone production.4 The histological types of Castleman disease (CD) typically include hyaline vascular, plasma cell, and mixed variants. The hyaline vascular type is the most common, accounting for approximately 90 % of CD cases, characterized by abnormal lymph node follicles and vascular proliferation in the interfollicular areas, usually without specific clinical symptoms. Clinically, Castleman disease (CD) is classified into unicentric CD (UCD) and multicentric CD (MCD). UCD is the most common form, while MCD has a poorer prognosis.5 On imaging, hyaline vascular type adrenal Castleman disease typically presents as a homogenous, well-defined soft tissue mass on CT. In contrast, plasma cell type UCD is less common, generally larger, and more likely to exhibit necrosis, peripheral enhancement, and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, indicating malignant features.6 In this case report, the adrenal tumor presented on CT as a homogeneous, well-defined mass with mild enhancement and calcification, with no tumors noted in other areas. Related adrenal hormone levels showed no significant abnormalities. Postoperative pathological and immunohistochemical results suggest unicentric hyaline vascular type Castleman disease.The treatment plan for Castleman disease (CD) should be determined based on the site of occurrence, tumor quantity, and pathological subtype, including options such as surgical intervention, chemotherapy, and targeted therapy. Surgical resection is the primary treatment option for adrenal Castleman disease (CD).7 If the lesion can be completely excised, prognosis is generally favorable regardless of pathological subtype, clinical presentation, or immunohistochemical results, and no adjuvant therapy is typically required.8 However, since a small subset of adrenal Castleman disease (CD) may present with catecholamine hormone abnormalities and hypertension, it is essential to preoperatively control blood pressure and heart rate using alpha-blockers in patients with these conditions.9 Additionally, studies have reported an increased risk of obstructive bronchiolitis, AA amyloidosis, and lymphoma in patients with Castleman disease (CD). Therefore, adrenal CD patients with persistent abnormal clinical symptoms, even after complete tumor resection, should undergo regular follow-up.10 Multicentric Castleman disease (MCD) is a more aggressive form of CD, typically presenting at an older age compared to unicentric Castleman disease (UCD). MCD is reported to be a potentially malignant condition with a generally poor prognosis. Although the optimal treatment approach remains undetermined, some success has been achieved with chemotherapy, systemic corticosteroids, radiotherapy, and combination therapies. However, the mortality rate remains high, approximately 50 %, indicating significant room for advancement in the management of MCD.114

Conclusion

Adrenal Castleman disease is a lymphoproliferative disorder occurring in the adrenal region, often difficult to differentiate from common adrenal tumors and lymphomas on imaging. Diagnosis is based on pathological and immunohistochemical findings. Surgical resection is the primary treatment modality with a favorable prognosis; however, regular follow-up is required for non-vascular type CD.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Ji Li: Conceptualization. Jing Bai: Methodology. Haifeng Wang: Writing – review & editing. Haole xu: Writing – original draft.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare no conflict of interest.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 50-year-old male.
- Chief Complaint: None (left adrenal mass found incidentally during routine examination).
- History of Present Illness: None.
- Past Medical History: No hypertension or diabetes; no prior abdominal surgeries.
- Personal History: None provided.
- Family History: None provided.
- Physical Examination: No abnormalities or hypertension noted.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Imaging: An abdominal CT scan showed a 55mm × 40mm left retroperitoneal mass with well-defined margins, calcifications, and mild enhancement. No lymphadenopathy or additional tumors detected.
  - Laboratory tests: Normal complete blood count, biochemistry, coagulation, myocardial markers, and adrenal hormone levels (renin, aldosterone, catecholamines, etc.).
  - Pathology and Immunochemistry: Post-surgery, the resected mass showed CD3 (++), CD20 (++), CD31 (+), CD34 (vascular +), KI-67 (10%), and characteristic histopathological features consistent with unicentric hyaline vascular type Castleman disease.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Primary differential consideration: Adrenal-origin tumors.**
   - **Rationale**: The patient presents with an isolated retroperitoneal mass; adrenal gland involvement was suspected based on imaging findings.
   - **Ruled out pheochromocytoma, aldosteronoma, and cortisol-secreting adenomas** due to normal adrenal hormone levels.

2. **Differentiation: Other retroperitoneal tumors or lymphoproliferative disorders.**
   - **Castleman disease (CD)** was considered due to the unique imaging findings (defined margins, calcifications), histological features (vascular proliferation, lymphoid structure), and lack of systemic lymphadenopathy or additional tumors on CT.
   - Non-hormonal activity and pathologic findings (hyaline vascular type UCD morphology) supported the diagnosis.
   - **Excluded malignancies like lymphoma or sarcoma** based on the absence of necrosis, typical malignancy features, and immunohistochemistry results.

3. **Final evaluation based on pathology and immunohistochemistry.**
   - Histological evidence of small vessel proliferation, lymphocyte arrangement, and immunohistochemical markers strongly indicated a unicentric hyaline vascular type Castleman disease.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Unicentric hyaline vascular type Castleman disease of the left adrenal region.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Unicentric hyaline vascular type Castleman disease.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Initial Assessment: The patient is a 50-year-old male presenting with an incidentally discovered left adrenal mass.  He is asymptomatic, and there is no reported history of present illness, hypertension, or diabetes. This immediately raises the question of the nature of the adrenal mass and whether it is hormonally active.

<step 2> Hormonal Function Evaluation:  Crucially, the laboratory tests reveal normal adrenal hormone levels (renin, aldosterone, catecholamines). This is significant as it strongly suggests that the adrenal mass is non-functional, ruling out common adrenal hormone-secreting tumors like pheochromocytoma, aldosteronoma, and cortisol-producing adenoma.

<step 3> Imaging Characteristics Analysis: The abdominal CT scan describes a left retroperitoneal mass with specific features: 55mm × 40mm size, well-defined margins, calcifications, and mild enhancement. These characteristics are not pathognomonic for any specific adrenal lesion but provide important morphological information. The absence of lymphadenopathy or additional tumors is also noted, suggesting a localized process.

<step 4> Surgical Intervention and Pathological Diagnosis:  The patient underwent surgical resection of the mass. This is the definitive step in diagnosis. The pathology report, including histopathology and immunochemistry, is the most critical piece of information for determining the final diagnosis.

<step 5> Pathology and Immunochemistry Interpretation: The pathology report reveals key findings: CD3 (++), CD20 (++), CD31 (+), CD34 (vascular +), KI-67 (10%), and "characteristic histopathological features consistent with unicentric hyaline vascular type Castleman disease."  CD3 and CD20 positivity indicate a mixed lymphoid population (T and B cells). CD31 and CD34 positivity, especially in a "vascular +" pattern, highlight vascular proliferation, a hallmark of hyaline vascular Castleman disease.  The KI-67 of 10% indicates a relatively low to moderate proliferation rate. The term "hyaline vascular type" is a specific subtype of Castleman disease. "Unicentric" further specifies the localized nature of the disease.

<step 6> Synthesis of Findings and Final Diagnosis: Combining the incidental discovery, normal hormone levels (ruling out functional adrenal tumors), imaging features, and, most importantly, the definitive pathology report confirming "unicentric hyaline vascular type Castleman disease," we can arrive at the final diagnosis. The location in the adrenal gland is unusual for Castleman disease, which typically involves lymph nodes, but extra-nodal presentations can occur. In this case, the retroperitoneal location of the mass and the absence of other lymphadenopathy further supports the "unicentric" nature described in the pathology report.

### Answer:
Unicentric Hyaline Vascular Type Castleman Disease of the Left Adrenal Gland

Hormonal Function Evaluation:  Crucially, the laboratory tests reveal normal adrenal hormone levels (renin, aldosterone, catecholamines). The KI-67 of 10% indicates a relatively low to moderate proliferation rate. The term "hyaline vascular type" is a specific subtype of Castleman disease. "Unicentric" further specifies the localized nature of the disease.

Synthesis of Findings and Final Diagnosis: Combining the incidental discovery, normal hormone levels (ruling out functional adrenal tumors), imaging features, and, most importantly, the definitive pathology report confirming "unicentric hyaline vascular type Castleman disease," we can arrive at the final diagnosis. The location in the adrenal gland is unusual for Castleman disease, which typically involves lymph nodes, but extra-nodal presentations can occur. gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Adrenal-origin tumors were primarily considered due to the patient's isolated retroperitoneal mass and imaging findings suggesting adrenal gland involvement.
<Step 2> Pheochromocytoma, aldosteronoma, and cortisol-secreting adenomas were ruled out due to normal adrenal hormone levels.

<Step 3> Castleman disease (CD) was considered given the unique imaging findings (defined margins, calcifications), histological features (vascular proliferation, lymphoid structure), and absence of systemic lymphadenopathy or additional tumors on CT.
<Step 4> Non-hormonal activity and hyaline vascular type unicentric Castleman disease (UCD) morphology supported the diagnosis.
<Step 5> Malignancies like lymphoma or sarcoma were excluded based on the absence of necrosis, typical malignancy features, and immunohistochemistry results.

<Step 6> Histological evidence of small vessel proliferation and lymphocyte arrangement, along with immunohistochemical markers, strongly indicated a unicentric hyaline vascular type Castleman disease.
Final diagnosis: Unicentric hyaline vascular type Castleman disease of the left adrenal region.
